name: Direction of centripetal force
question_text: An object moves in a circle at constant speed. In what direction does the net force on the object point?
general_feedback: Uniform circular motion requires an acceleration directed toward the centre of the circle (centripetal acceleration). By Newton's second law the net force is in that same direction — toward the centre.
correct_feedback: Correct — Centripetal means 'centre-seeking' — the net force is radially inward.

answers.text: Toward the centre of the circle
answers.feedback: Centripetal means 'centre-seeking' — the net force is radially inward.

answers.text: Away from the centre of the circle
answers.feedback: An outward force would push the object away; circular motion requires a centre-directed net force.
A force along the velocity would speed the object up; at constant speed the net force must be perpendicular to the velocity.

answers.text: Opposite to the direction of motion
answers.feedback: That would slow the object; at constant speed the net force is perpendicular to the velocity, not anti-parallel to it.
